title: Two-Variable Data
blurb: Scatterplots, the correlation coefficient, the least-squares line, and why correlation is not causation.

lessons.title: Scatterplots: Data Grows a Second Dimension
lessons.concreteComparison: Does studying more actually raise scores? Ask $20$ students for two numbers each — hours studied, exam score — and plot each student as a POINT: hours across, score up. The cloud of $20$ points answers at a glance what $40$ separate numbers never could: the cloud drifts upward. More hours, higher scores, visible.
lessons.objective: Plot paired data as scatterplots, and describe associations by direction, form, and strength.
concept: A **scatterplot** puts one variable on each axis and one POINT per individual. The **explanatory** variable (the one you think does the influencing — hours) takes the x-axis; the **response** (scores) takes y.

Describe the cloud with three words: **direction** (positive drift up-right, negative drift down-right, or none), **form** (linear cloud vs curved vs clustered), **strength** (tight cloud vs loose scatter).
Plus the ever-present fourth: **unusual points** — outliers standing off the cloud, which in two dimensions can be extreme in x, in y, or off the PATTERN while ordinary in both coordinates.
The direction-form-strength description is Unit 9's SCSO ritual, rebuilt for pairs: say all three (plus outliers), in context, before any number gets computed.
lessons.keyIdea: One point per individual, explanatory on x; describe direction, form, strength — and flag points off the pattern.

facts.title: The axes
facts.explanation: The suspected influencer goes across; the outcome goes up.

facts.title: The description
facts.explanation: Three words and a flag — the two-variable SCSO.

workedExamples.statement: Hours studied vs exam score for $20$ students: the cloud drifts up-right, roughly straight, moderately tight. Describe the association — and who goes on which axis?
workedExamples.solution: Hours (suspected influencer) on x, score on y. Description: "a moderately strong, positive, linear association — students who studied more tended to score higher." Tendency, not law: the cloud has thickness.

workedExamples.statement: Outdoor temperature vs hot-chocolate sales at a kiosk: describe the expected cloud.
workedExamples.solution: Negative direction (colder days, more sales — the cloud drifts DOWN-right), roughly linear over normal temperatures, fairly strong. Note the explanatory choice: temperature influences sales, never the reverse.

workedExamples.statement: Height vs age for people aged $2$ to $40$: why does 'linear' fail as a description?
workedExamples.solution: The cloud rises steeply through childhood then FLATTENS after ~$18$ — a curved (two-regime) form. A single line would misdescribe both phases; form is the word that catches it. (Curved clouds get curved tools — or split ranges.)

commonMistakes.correction: Convention carries meaning: x is the suspected influencer, y the outcome. Swapping axes muddles every downstream slope interpretation.
"Positive" alone hides whether the cloud is a tight line or a vague mist, straight or bent. Direction AND form AND strength — three words minimum.

tryIt.statement: Number of practice free throws per week vs game free-throw percentage: predict the cloud's three-word description.
tryIt.solution: Positive, roughly linear, moderate strength (talent and luck add scatter). Practice on x.

tryIt.statement: Shoe size vs score on a spelling test for adults: predict the cloud.
tryIt.solution: No direction — a shapeless blob. Two unrelated variables produce exactly that, and the plot proves it faster than any argument.

steps.title: Pairs, plotted
beats: Twenty students, two numbers each: hours studied, exam score.
One POINT per student: hours across, score up.
The cloud drifts up-right — the answer, visible.
Two-variable data asks: how do they move TOGETHER?

steps.title: Direction, form, strength
beats: DIRECTION: up-right (+), down-right (−), or none.
FORM: linear cloud, curve, clusters.
STRENGTH: tight (points hug the trend) vs loose mist.
Plus outliers: points off the PATTERN, even if ordinary in x and y alone.

steps.title: Data becomes dots
steps.teach: Each student is one dot, placed at (hours studied, quiz score). Twelve students, twelve dots — and already a pattern the table hid: the cloud climbs. Two-variable data is about **pairs**, and the scatterplot is the picture of every pair at once.

steps.title: Who goes on x?
steps.prompt: Plotting fertilizer amount vs tomato yield: which goes on the x-axis?
steps.explanation: The explanatory variable (what you control or suspect drives the outcome) takes x; the response takes y. The choice sets up every later slope sentence: 'per extra kilogram of fertilizer...'.

steps.title: Name that association
steps.prompt: Car weight vs fuel efficiency: the cloud drifts down-right, fairly tight, straight. Description?
steps.explanation: Heavier cars, fewer km per liter: negative direction; tight and straight: strong and linear. Three words, and an engineer's design constraint appears in the data.

steps.title: Off-pattern beats off-scale
steps.body: In two dimensions a point can be unremarkable in x AND in y yet scream from the plot — a student with average hours and an average score doesn't exist in a cloud where those two never co-occur. Scan for off-PATTERN points, not just extreme coordinates; they're where the best stories (and data errors) hide.

steps.title: The first scatterplot mapped stars
steps.body: John Herschel drew what historians call the first true scatterplot in 1833 — plotting double stars' orbital positions. Fifty years later Francis Galton scattered parents' heights against children's and noticed the cloud's tilt was less than 1:1 — children of tall parents were tall, but less so. He named the effect 'regression', and the next lessons carry his word.

steps.title: What you learned
points: One point per pair; explanatory on x, response on y.
Describe with direction + form + strength, in context.
Flag off-pattern points — 2D outliers can hide in ordinary coordinates.

lessons.title: The Correlation Coefficient
lessons.concreteComparison: "Moderately tight" is your word against mine. The **correlation coefficient** $r$ ends the argument with one number between $-1$ and $+1$: sign gives the direction, magnitude gives the tightness. Hours-vs-scores at $r = 0.8$ and temperature-vs-sales at $r = -0.9$ are now comparable claims — the second cloud is tighter, whatever the units.
lessons.objective: Interpret r: sign, magnitude, unitlessness, and its blind spots (nonlinearity, outlier sensitivity).
concept: **What $r$ measures:** the direction and tightness of a LINEAR association, on a fixed scale: $r = +1$ (perfect uphill line), $0$ (no linear pattern), $-1$ (perfect downhill line). Working bands: $|r| > 0.8$ strong, $0.5$–$0.8$ moderate, below $0.3$ weak.

$r$ is built from paired z-scores — roughly the average of $z_x \cdot z_y$ across points — which makes it UNIT-FREE and symmetric: correlation of height with weight equals weight with height, in inches or centimeters, points or percents.
**Blind spot 1 — nonlinearity:** $r$ only sees lines. A perfect U-shaped curve can score $r \approx 0$: strong association, invisible to $r$. Plot FIRST; $r$ second.

**Blind spot 2 — outliers:** built from means and SDs (Unit 9's sensitive tools), $r$ can be dragged wildly by one off-pattern point — a single distant point can fake a strong correlation or destroy a real one. The plot, again, is the audit.
lessons.keyIdea: r ∈ [−1, 1]: sign = direction, magnitude = linear tightness; unit-free — but blind to curves and fragile to outliers, so plot first.

facts.title: The scale
facts.explanation: Paired z-scores make it unit-free; ±1 are perfect lines, 0 is no linear pattern.

facts.title: The blind spots
facts.explanation: r sees only lines and uses sensitive ingredients — the scatterplot audits it.

workedExamples.statement: Match the descriptions to $r$ values $\{-0.9, -0.3, 0.05, 0.6\}$: (a) tight downhill cloud; (b) loose downhill mist; (c) shapeless blob; (d) clear but scattered uphill drift.
workedExamples.solution: (a) $-0.9$ — strong negative. (b) $-0.3$ — weak negative. (c) $0.05$ — essentially no linear pattern. (d) $0.6$ — moderate positive. Sign reads direction; magnitude reads tightness.

workedExamples.statement: Height-vs-weight gives $r = 0.65$ measured in cm and kg. The researchers convert to inches and pounds. What happens to $r$ — and to the claim it makes?
workedExamples.solution: Nothing: $r$ is built from z-scores, and units cancel in standardization (Unit 10's whole point). $r = 0.65$ in any units — a moderate positive linear association either way.

workedExamples.statement: Speed vs fuel efficiency: efficiency RISES up to $\sim 80$ km/h then FALLS beyond — a clean inverted U. A student computes $r \approx 0.02$ and reports "no association." Diagnose.
workedExamples.solution: The association is strong — but curved, and $r$ only detects LINES. The uphill half and downhill half cancel to near-zero. The plot shows what the number can't; "no LINEAR association" is the only claim $r \approx 0$ ever supports.

commonMistakes.correction: r = 0 means no LINEAR relationship. U-shapes, cycles, and clusters can all hide behind it. The scatterplot has veto power.

commonMistakes.text: Treating r as a slope or a percentage.
commonMistakes.correction: r = 0.8 doesn't mean 'y rises 0.8 per x' (that's slope, next lesson) or '80% of anything'. It means: tight uphill cloud. Tightness, not steepness.

tryIt.statement: Rank by strength of linear association: $r = -0.85$, $r = 0.4$, $r = 0.7$.
tryIt.solution: By $|r|$: $0.85 > 0.7 > 0.4$ — the negative one is the STRONGEST. Sign is direction, never strength.

tryIt.statement: A dataset of $12$ points has $r = 0.2$; removing one weird point yields $r = 0.75$. What's the story?
tryIt.solution: One off-pattern outlier was strangling a real moderate-to-strong association. Investigate that point (typo? different population?) and report both r's with the finding — never silently pick the prettier one.

steps.title: Tightness, standardized
beats: "Moderately tight" — says who? Enter $r$.
$+1$: perfect uphill line. $-1$: perfect downhill. $0$: no linear pattern.
Sign $=$ direction. Magnitude $=$ tightness.
Bands: $|r| > 0.8$ strong · $0.5$–$0.8$ moderate · $< 0.3$ weak.

steps.title: Paired z-scores
beats: $r \approx$ the average of $z_x \cdot z_y$ across the points.
Above-average x WITH above-average y → positive products → positive $r$.
Units cancel in the z's: $r$ is unit-free and symmetric.
cm/kg or inches/pounds: same $r$, same claim.

steps.title: One cloud, three stories
steps.teach: Swap the dataset and watch every point glide. Positive: the cloud climbs — more $x$ tends to mean more $y$. Negative: it sinks. None: knowing $x$ tells you nothing. Correlation is the **direction and tightness** of the cloud, and you should be able to call it by eye before touching a formula.

steps.title: Which is strongest?
steps.prompt: Which correlation indicates the STRONGEST linear association: $r = -0.92$, $r = 0.85$, or $r = 0.15$?
steps.explanation: Strength is $|r|$: $0.92 > 0.85 > 0.15$. The minus sign describes the downhill direction, not weakness — the most common misread on this entire topic.

steps.title: What does r = 0 permit?
steps.prompt: Given only "$r = 0.01$", which conclusion is safe?
options: No LINEAR association — but curves, clusters remain possible
steps.explanation: r speaks only of lines. A perfect parabola, two clusters, a cycle — all can produce r ≈ 0 while being strongly related. Only the plot can grant 'unrelated'.

steps.title: Plot, then r, then together
steps.body: Professionals always report the scatterplot AND r as a pair: the plot audits the number (curves? outliers?), the number calibrates the plot (is 'tight' 0.7 or 0.95?). Either alone can mislead; together they're honest.

steps.title: The datasaurus
steps.body: Statisticians built a dataset shaped like a DINOSAUR with the same means, SDs, and r as a boring cloud — then morphed it into a star, circles, and parallel lines, all sharing identical summary statistics. 'The Datasaurus Dozen' (2017) is Anscombe's quartet for the meme age, and its moral is this lesson's: plot first, always.

steps.title: What you learned
points: r ∈ [−1,1]: sign = direction, |r| = linear tightness, units irrelevant.
r = 0 rules out lines only; one outlier can hijack everything.
Report plot + r as a pair — each audits the other.

lessons.title: The Least-Squares Line
lessons.concreteComparison: The study-hours cloud clearly drifts upward — so WHICH line through it should make predictions? Tilt any candidate line and measure each point's vertical miss; square the misses, add them up. The **least-squares line** is the tilt-and-height that makes that sum smallest — the provably best straight summary, and now a prediction machine: $\hat{y} = 42 + 5.2x$ turns "I'll study 6 hours" into "expect about 73."
lessons.objective: Interpret and use the least-squares line ŷ = a + bx: slope and intercept in context, and predictions within range.
concept: The **least-squares line** minimizes the sum of SQUARED vertical misses (residuals) — squaring for the same reason variance did (Unit 9): no cancellation, big misses punished. The hat in $\hat{y}$ means PREDICTED, distinguishing forecasts from observed values.

**Slope $b$** is the workhorse sentence: "per one-unit increase in $x$, predicted $y$ changes by $b$" — per extra study hour, about $5.2$ more points, ON AVERAGE. It's a claim about the cloud's drift, not about any single student.
**Intercept $a$** is the prediction at $x = 0$ — sometimes meaningful (a zero-study baseline of $42$), often just the line's anchor (predicted weight at height zero is geometry, not biology).

Two anchors worth knowing: the line always passes through $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}$) — the cloud's center of mass — and its slope carries $r$'s sign (uphill clouds get uphill lines). The line and the correlation tell one story in two dialects.
lessons.keyIdea: ŷ = a + bx minimizes squared vertical misses; slope = predicted change per unit of x (in context), intercept = the x = 0 anchor, and the line passes through the cloud's center.

facts.title: The line
facts.explanation: The tilt-and-height with the smallest total squared miss — the best straight summary.

facts.title: Through the center
facts.explanation: The least-squares line pivots on the cloud's center of mass.

workedExamples.statement: The study line: $\hat{y} = 42 + 5.2x$ (score from hours). Interpret both coefficients and predict the score for $6$ hours.
workedExamples.solution: Slope: each extra study hour predicts about $5.2$ more points, on average. Intercept: a non-studier is predicted $42$ — here meaningful (the no-prep baseline). At $x = 6$: $\hat{y} = 42 + 5.2 \times 6 = 73.2$ — expect around $73$.

workedExamples.statement: Ana studied $5$ hours and scored $75$. The line predicts $\hat{y} = 42 + 26 = 68$. Compute and interpret her residual.
workedExamples.solution: Residual $= y - \hat{y} = 75 - 68 = +7$: Ana beat her prediction by $7$ points — she sits $7$ above the line. Positive residuals overperform the trend; negative underperform.

workedExamples.statement: The hours in the data run from $0$ to $8$. A classmate plugs in $x = 25$ hours and announces $\hat{y} = 172$ points on a $100$-point exam. Name the crime.
workedExamples.solution: **Extrapolation**: the line summarizes the cloud where the cloud EXISTS ($0$–$8$ hours). Beyond it, the linear pattern is pure assumption — and here it predicts an impossible score, exposing itself. Predictions live within the data's range; outside is fiction with decimal places.

commonMistakes.text: Reading the slope as a guarantee per student.
commonMistakes.correction: b = 5.2 describes the cloud's average drift, not a personal contract — individual residuals scatter around it. 'Predicts, on average' is the honest verb.
The line knows the cloud, not the universe. Extrapolation (25 hours → 172 points) manufactures nonsense confidently. Check the range before plugging in.

tryIt.statement: Ice-cream stand: $\hat{y} = -30 + 6x$ (sales in cones from temperature °C, data range $15$–$35$°). Predict sales at $25$° and interpret the slope.
tryIt.solution: $\hat{y} = -30 + 150 = 120$ cones. Slope: each extra degree predicts $6$ more cones on average. (The intercept at $0°$ is an anchor, not a claim — no one sells $-30$ cones.)

tryIt.statement: A point has $y = 50$ and $\hat{y} = 58$. Find the residual and place the point relative to the line.
tryIt.solution: $50 - 58 = -8$: eight units BELOW the line — an underperformer relative to trend.

steps.title: Which line through the cloud?
beats: Any tilted line makes vertical MISSES at each point.
Square the misses (no cancellation, big ones punished), sum.
The least-squares line: the tilt-and-height minimizing that sum.
Provably best straight summary — and a prediction machine.

steps.title: Beat the residuals
steps.teach: Steer the slope and intercept to shrink the dashed **misses** — each stick is one point's residual. Your score is the average miss; press **snap to best fit** to see the least-squares line, the one no line can beat. Regression isn't magic: it's this game, solved exactly.

steps.title: Say it in context
steps.prompt: $\hat{y} = 12 + 0.4x$ predicts plant height (cm) from sunlight (hours/day). The slope sentence is…
options: "Each extra daily sunlight hour predicts 0.4 cm more height, on average"
"Plants grow 12 cm per hour of sunlight"
steps.explanation: Slope = predicted change in y per unit of x, spoken in units, with 'on average'. The 12 is the intercept (zero-sun anchor); percentages belong to neither coefficient.

steps.title: Residuals: signed misses
beats: Residual $= y - \hat{y}$: observed minus predicted.
Positive: above the line (overperformed the trend).
Negative: below (underperformed).
The line is BUILT to make the squared residuals as small as possible.

steps.title: Legal predictions
steps.prompt: The cone line ($\hat{y} = -30 + 6x$) was fit on temperatures $15$–$35$°C. Which prediction is LEGAL?
options: Sales at $30$° — inside the data's range
Sales at $50$° — just extend the line
steps.explanation: The line summarizes the cloud where data exists. At $-5°$ it predicts negative cones (self-refuting); at $50°$ the linearity is pure faith (and the stand may have melted). Interpolate freely; extrapolate never — or loudly.

steps.title: The line pivots on the mean point
steps.body: The least-squares line always passes through (x̄, ȳ) — the cloud's center of mass — and its slope shares r's sign. Quick audit for any reported line: plug in x̄; if ȳ doesn't come out, the fit (or the report) is wrong.

steps.title: Least squares found a planet
steps.body: When the asteroid Ceres vanished behind the sun in 1801, 24-year-old Gauss used least-squares fitting on its few observed positions to predict where it would reappear — and it did, within half a degree. The method that grades your scatterplot homework re-found a world.

steps.title: What you learned
points: ŷ = a + bx minimizes squared misses; hat means predicted.
Slope: change per unit of x, on average, in context; intercept: the x=0 anchor.
Residual = y − ŷ; predict inside the data's range only.

lessons.title: How Good Is the Line?
lessons.concreteComparison: Two stands both fit sales lines. Stand A's points hug its line; Stand B's spray around it. Same slopes, same intercepts — completely different trust. The line's quality lives in what it LEAVES BEHIND: the residuals. Small, patternless leftovers mean the line earned its predictions; big or curved leftovers mean the line is hiding something.
lessons.objective: Judge fit quality with residuals: typical miss size, leftover patterns, r² as explained variation, and influential points.
concept: **Typical miss:** the spread of residuals (their SD, informally "the typical prediction error") is the line's honesty label: "predictions typically miss by $\pm 6$ points" tells a user everything a slope doesn't.

**Leftover patterns:** residuals should look like patternless noise around zero. A CURVE in the residuals means the straight line missed a bend (fit something curved); a FAN (spread growing with $x$) means predictions get less reliable at one end. Structure in the leftovers is unfinished business.

**$r^2$** (the square of the correlation) is the standard fit score: the fraction of $y$'s variation the line accounts for. $r = 0.8 \to r^2 = 0.64$: the line explains $64\%$ of score variation; the rest lives in the residuals. It's a share of variation explained — not a probability, not a grade.

**Influential points:** an extreme-$x$ point acts as a lever on the line — one such point can seize the slope. Fit-with and fit-without, compare, and report the difference honestly (Unit 9's flag-investigate-decide, now for lines).
lessons.keyIdea: Judge the line by its leftovers: typical miss size, no leftover pattern, r² as the share of variation explained, and lever points checked with/without.

facts.title: r-squared
facts.latex: r^2 = \text{share of } y\text{'s variation the line explains}
facts.explanation: r = 0.8 → 64% explained; the residuals own the rest.

facts.title: Residual health
facts.explanation: Curves or fans in the leftovers mean the model missed structure.

workedExamples.statement: The study line has $r = 0.8$. Compute and interpret $r^2$.
workedExamples.solution: $r^2 = 0.64$: hours studied account for $64\%$ of the variation in scores; the remaining $36\%$ — sleep, talent, luck, question choice — lives in the residuals. A solid but humble line.

workedExamples.statement: Two models predict delivery time. Model 1: typical residual $\pm 3$ min. Model 2: typical residual $\pm 15$ min. Same slope sentences. Which do you ship, and what does the number mean to a customer?
workedExamples.solution: Model 1 — its promises land within $\pm 3$ minutes, typically. To a customer, THE RESIDUAL SPREAD is the product: "your food arrives 30–36 min from now" versus "15–45 min". Slopes describe trends; residual spread describes trust.

workedExamples.statement: A growth line fits age-vs-height for children $2$–$16$, but its residuals curve: positive at both ends, negative in the middle. Diagnose and prescribe.
workedExamples.solution: The residual U-shape says the true relationship BENDS (growth isn't constant-rate) — the straight line underpredicts young and old, overpredicts the middle. Prescription: fit a curve, or split the age range. The r may still look decent — residual PLOTS catch what r misses.

commonMistakes.text: Reading r² = 0.64 as '64% accurate' or 'correct 64% of the time'.
commonMistakes.correction: r² is a share of VARIATION explained — a variance-accounting number, not an accuracy rate or probability. 'Explains 64% of the variation in y' is the only faithful sentence.

commonMistakes.text: Judging fit by r alone, skipping the residual plot.
commonMistakes.correction: A bent relationship can post a healthy r while systematically mispredicting entire ranges. Leftovers, plotted, are the true report card.

tryIt.statement: A line has $r = -0.9$. What share of variation does it explain — and does the minus sign reduce it?
tryIt.solution: $r^2 = 0.81$: $81\%$ explained. Squaring kills the sign — downhill lines explain just as well.

tryIt.statement: A fit's residuals fan out — tiny for small $x$, huge for large $x$. What should the report say?
tryIt.solution: "Predictions are reliable at low $x$ (typical miss small) and increasingly unreliable at high $x$" — one line, two trust levels, disclosed. A single overall miss-number would hide the fan.

steps.title: Judge the leftovers
beats: Two identical-looking lines, different clouds: one hugs, one sprays.
The line's quality is in what it LEAVES BEHIND.
Small, patternless residuals: the line earned its keep.
Big or structured residuals: unfinished business.

steps.title: One point off the story
steps.teach: Eleven points tell a clean climbing story — and one sits far below the pack. Outliers in two-variable data can drag the fitted line and inflate your confidence in nonsense. Before trusting any line or any $r$, look at the cloud and ask who's not following the plot.

steps.title: Square it
steps.prompt: A line has $r = 0.5$. The share of variation it explains is…
steps.explanation: $r^2 = 0.25$: a moderate-LOOKING correlation explains only a quarter of the variation. Squaring is humbling — which is exactly its job.

steps.title: Healthy or hiding?
steps.prompt: A residual plot shows points scattered evenly above and below zero, no shape, similar spread everywhere. Verdict?
options: Healthy — the line captured the pattern; leftovers are noise
Unhealthy — residuals should all be zero
steps.explanation: Patternless, even scatter around zero is EXACTLY what finished models leave behind. Zero residuals would mean suspicious perfection; trending residuals would mean missed structure.

steps.title: Influential points
beats: A point far out in $x$ is a LEVER on the line.
One lever can seize the slope — with the other points as passengers.
Audit: fit WITH and WITHOUT, compare the slopes.
Big change → report both fits and investigate the point. (Flag → investigate → decide.)

steps.title: A complete line report
steps.body: Ship four things with every fit: the equation (with slope sentence), r², the typical miss size, and one sentence on the residual plot's health. Thirty seconds more than the equation alone — and the difference between a homework answer and an analysis.

steps.title: Moneyball was an r² argument
steps.body: The Oakland A's famous analytics revolution boiled down to fit quality: on-base percentage explained more variance in runs scored (higher r²) than the batting average scouts revered. Choosing the predictor with better leftovers, the poorest team in baseball made the playoffs — and every sport now employs residual-readers.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Fit = leftovers: typical miss size is the trust number.
r² = share of variation explained (not accuracy, not probability).
Patterned residuals mean missed structure; lever points get with/without audits.

lessons.title: Correlation Is Not Causation
lessons.concreteComparison: Cities' ice-cream sales correlate beautifully with their drowning deaths — $r$ near $0.9$ across summer weeks. Ban ice cream, save swimmers? The real driver stands behind both: HOT WEATHER sends people to buy cones AND to swim. A **lurking variable** manufactured the correlation, and no r-value, however large, can rule such a puppeteer out.
lessons.objective: Explain why correlation alone can't establish causation, identify lurking variables and other explanations, and know what evidence CAN support causal claims.
concept: A correlation between $A$ and $B$ has (at least) four live explanations: $A$ causes $B$; $B$ causes $A$ (reverse causation); a lurking variable $C$ drives both; or coincidence (small samples and many-variable fishing produce phantom r's). The data pattern alone cannot pick the winner.

**Lurking variables** are the classic: heat drives cones and drownings; family wealth drives both tutoring and test scores; sicker patients get both more medication and worse outcomes (making medicine look harmful).
**Reverse causation** hides in plausible-sounding claims: confidence correlates with success — which causes which? The scatterplot has no arrow.

What CAN support causation: a **randomized experiment** — randomly assigning who gets the treatment breaks every lurking variable's grip (random groups match on wealth, health, weather, everything). That's Unit 12's centerpiece. Until then, the honest sentence is: "$A$ and $B$ are associated" — and the honest instinct is to hunt for $C$.
lessons.keyIdea: Four explanations for every correlation: A→B, B→A, lurking C, or chance. Only randomized assignment can cut through — until then, say 'associated'.

facts.title: The four suspects
facts.latex: A \to B, \quad B \to A, \quad C \to A \text{ and } B, \quad \text{chance}
facts.explanation: Every correlation carries all four until design (not data volume) eliminates them.

facts.title: The cure
facts.explanation: Randomized experiments break the puppeteer's strings — Unit 12's story.

workedExamples.statement: Ice-cream sales and drownings: $r \approx 0.9$ across weeks. Name the lurking variable and the test that would expose it.
workedExamples.solution: Hot weather drives both. Exposure test: hold temperature fixed (compare only equally-hot weeks) — within a temperature band, the cone-drowning correlation collapses. A lurking variable's signature: the correlation dies when you control for it.

workedExamples.statement: "Students with tutors score higher — tutoring works!" Give the lurking-variable and reverse-causation counter-stories.
workedExamples.solution: Lurking: family wealth buys tutors AND quieter study rooms, better schools, less part-time work — wealth may drive both. Reverse: struggling students HIRE tutors (which would produce a NEGATIVE correlation, so observing positive r already mixes selection stories). Only random assignment of tutoring can isolate its effect.

workedExamples.statement: A hospital's data: patients given the strongest medication die more often. Should the medication be banned?
workedExamples.solution: The lurking variable is SEVERITY: the sickest patients receive the strongest drugs and also die more — sickness drives both. (This exact pattern, called confounding by indication, is why observational medical data can make effective treatments look lethal, and why drug approval requires randomized trials.)

commonMistakes.text: Letting a big r settle a causal argument.
commonMistakes.correction: Strength of association ≠ evidence of mechanism: the cone-drowning r is huge and means nothing causal. Size impresses; design convinces.
Association is real information — it predicts (cones DO forecast drowning risk), flags hypotheses, and guides where to run experiments. It just can't assign blame by itself.

tryIt.statement: Countries' chocolate consumption correlates with their Nobel prize counts. Offer the lurking variable.
tryIt.solution: National wealth: rich countries buy more chocolate AND fund more research. (A real published correlation, r ≈ 0.79 — and a real teaching classic.)

tryIt.statement: "Kids who sleep with the light on become nearsighted more often" made headlines. Later research found nearsighted PARENTS both pass on myopia and prefer lit rooms. Classify the original error.
tryIt.solution: Lurking variable (parental myopia) driving both the light and the eyesight — the light was innocent. A famous real case of association mistaken for cause.

steps.title: Ban ice cream, save swimmers?
beats: Cones and drownings: $r \approx 0.9$ across summer weeks.
The puppeteer: HOT WEATHER, driving both.
No r-value, however large, can rule out a lurking variable.
The correlation is real. The causation is manufactured.

steps.title: Four suspects, every time
beats: $A \to B$: maybe.
$B \to A$: reverse — the scatterplot has no arrow.
$C \to$ both: the lurking variable.
Chance: small samples fishing many variables catch phantom r's.

steps.title: Firefighters and damage
steps.prompt: Fires attended by MORE firefighters do more damage ($r > 0$). The best explanation is…
options: Lurking variable: fire SIZE drives both the crew count and the damage
steps.explanation: Big fires summon big crews AND destroy more — severity is the puppeteer. (The same skeleton as strong-medication-kills: dose follows severity.) Blaming the responders reads the arrow backward through a lurker.

steps.title: What CAN prove it?
steps.prompt: Which evidence would genuinely support "tutoring raises scores"?
options: Randomly ASSIGN tutoring to half of the volunteers, compare groups
steps.explanation: Random assignment balances wealth, motivation, prior grades — every lurker, known and unknown — between groups. Scaling up an observational study scales up the SAME bias; a bigger r measures the bias more precisely. Design beats data volume.

steps.title: Say 'associated', hunt for C
steps.body: Until design justifies more: "A and B are associated." Then earn your keep by proposing the C's — weather, wealth, severity, age — and checking whether controlling for them kills the correlation. The proposal-and-check habit is most of practical data literacy.

steps.title: Spurious correlations, the website
steps.body: Tyler Vigen's 'Spurious Correlations' catalogs phantom r's mined from public data: US cheese consumption tracks deaths by bedsheet entanglement (r = 0.95); Nicolas Cage films track pool drownings (r = 0.67). Ten thousand variables, cross-correlated, GUARANTEE absurd matches — the 'chance' suspect, industrialized into comedy.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Every correlation: A→B, B→A, lurking C, or chance — data alone can't pick.
Lurkers die under control; causation needs random assignment (Unit 12).
Say 'associated'; propose the C's; respect prediction even without causation.

lessons.title: Capstone: The Full Two-Variable Analysis
lessons.concreteComparison: A café owner hands you a year of data: daily temperature and daily iced-drink sales. "Should I staff by the forecast?" One question, every tool: plot the cloud, describe it, quote $r$, fit the line, interpret the slope in drinks-per-degree, check the residuals, refuse to extrapolate past the observed temperatures, and keep the causal language honest. That deliverable — start to finish — is this capstone.
lessons.objective: Run complete two-variable analyses: plot → describe → r → line → residuals → prediction with range limits → honest causal framing.
concept: The full pipeline: (1) PLOT the scatterplot; (2) DESCRIBE direction-form-strength + outliers; (3) QUOTE $r$ (with the plot as audit); (4) FIT the least-squares line if form is linear; (5) INTERPRET slope (and intercept if meaningful) in context; (6) CHECK residuals (typical miss, patterns, levers); (7) PREDICT within range; (8) FRAME causally with honesty.

Each step gates the next: curved form (step 2) halts the linear fit; a lever point (step 6) demands a with/without audit; residual fans qualify the predictions; and step 8's language never exceeds the design's warrant.

The deliverable is a PARAGRAPH, not a number: equation, slope sentence, $r^2$, typical miss, range limits, and the associated-not-caused disclaimer where due — the exact report a café owner, coach, or clinic can act on.

This closes the descriptive arc: Units 9–11 describe one variable, locate values, and relate pairs. What remains is the biggest question: when do patterns in a SAMPLE license claims about the WORLD? Unit 12 — the capstone of the course.
lessons.keyIdea: Plot → describe → r → fit → interpret → residuals → bounded predictions → honest causal language: eight gated steps, one actionable paragraph.

facts.title: The pipeline
facts.explanation: Each step gates the next; the paragraph at the end is the deliverable.

facts.title: The two disciplines
facts.explanation: Range limits and causal honesty — the marks of a professional analysis.

workedExamples.statement: Café data ($60$ days, temps $18$–$36$°C): cloud positive-linear-strong, $r = 0.88$, line $\hat{y} = -50 + 9x$ (drinks from °C), residuals patternless, typical miss $\pm 15$ drinks. Write the owner's paragraph.
workedExamples.solution: "Iced-drink sales track temperature strongly ($r = 0.88$; the line explains $r^2 \approx 77\%$ of day-to-day variation). Expect about $9$ extra drinks per degree: a $30°$ forecast predicts $-50 + 270 = 220$ drinks, typically within $\pm 15$. Valid for forecasts between $18$ and $36°$ — beyond that we have no data. Temperature needn't CAUSE every sale, but as a staffing forecast the association is exactly what you need."

workedExamples.statement: Midway through the analysis, one day shows $35°$ and only $12$ drinks (a pipe burst; the café closed early). It sits far below the line. Apply the pipeline's discipline.
workedExamples.solution: Step 6 catches it: a large negative residual — investigate → cause identified (closure, not weather). Disposition: exclude it from the FIT with a documented note (it doesn't represent temperature-driven demand), and report the fit's stability: slope with/without. Never silently delete; never blindly keep a known non-representative point.

workedExamples.statement: The owner asks: "So heat CAUSES my sales — should I install heaters outside in winter?" Answer with the unit's full honesty.
workedExamples.solution: Two disciplines at once. Causal: the association is consistent with heat driving thirst, but the data can't isolate it (season, tourists, holidays all move with temperature — lurking C's). Range: winter temps sit far OUTSIDE $18$–$36°$ — the line knows nothing there, and heaters wouldn't recreate summer anyway. Verdict: staff by the forecast (prediction is licensed); don't re-engineer the climate (causation and extrapolation are not).

commonMistakes.text: Skipping steps because the data 'looks fine'.
commonMistakes.correction: The pipeline's gates exist because each catches a different failure: the plot catches curves, residuals catch levers and fans, range-checks catch fiction. Fine-looking data is how all of them sneak through.

commonMistakes.text: Delivering the equation instead of the paragraph.
commonMistakes.correction: ŷ = −50 + 9x is homework. The paragraph — slope sentence, trust interval, range limits, causal framing — is what someone can ACT on. Analyses end in sentences.

tryIt.statement: Fit: $\hat{y} = 20 + 0.5x$ (game score from practice minutes, data range $30$–$120$ min, $r = 0.6$). Predict for $80$ minutes, give $r^2$, and state one caveat.
tryIt.solution: $\hat{y} = 20 + 40 = 60$. $r^2 = 0.36$ — practice explains about a third of score variation. Caveat menu: typical miss unknown without residuals; prediction valid only in $30$–$120$; association ≠ causation (better players may simply practice more).

tryIt.statement: Your line predicts a student with $0$ absences scores $85$, and each absence predicts $-3$ points (data: $0$–$15$ absences). A colleague wants the predicted score for $40$ absences. Respond.
tryIt.solution: $85 - 120 = -35$: an impossible score, because $40$ absences is far outside the observed $0$–$15$ — extrapolation self-destructs again. Report: "no data beyond 15 absences; the linear pattern is unverified there and the prediction is meaningless."

steps.title: One year of café data
beats: "Should I staff by the forecast?"
Plot. Describe. Quote $r$. Fit. Interpret. Check leftovers.
Predict — inside the range. Frame — without causal overreach.
Eight steps, one actionable paragraph. That's the job.

steps.title: Which gate fires?
steps.prompt: Mid-pipeline, the scatterplot shows a clear arch (rises then falls). What happens next?
options: Halt the LINEAR fit — the form gate failed; fit a curve or split the range
steps.explanation: Step 2's form check gates step 4's fit: a straight line through an arch mispredicts everywhere while possibly posting a bland r. The gates aren't bureaucracy — each one catches a specific disaster.

steps.title: Licensed claims
steps.prompt: From observational café data with $r = 0.88$, which claim is fully licensed?
options: "Hot days are associated with higher sales; forecast-based staffing is justified"
"Heat causes sales — guaranteed 9 drinks per degree"
steps.explanation: Association + prediction-in-range: licensed. 'Causes' + 'guaranteed': exceeds the design (observational) and the noise (±15). 60°C: exceeds the range (and the planet's café weather). One claim survives all the gates.

steps.title: The last question
steps.body: You can describe, locate, relate, and predict. One question remains — the biggest: when does a SAMPLE's pattern license a claim about the WORLD? Sampling, experiments, and the logic of inference. Unit 12 closes the course where working statisticians spend their days.

steps.title: Weather-elastic everything
steps.body: Retail chains run temperature-regression exactly like the café: one grocery giant found barbecue sales jump nonlinearly above 24°C while soup collapses; a UK chain stocks stores daily off weather-regression forecasts. Somewhere, a supply chain just ordered 9 extra drinks per degree — your capstone paragraph, at industrial scale.

steps.title: What you learned — Unit 11 complete
points: Eight gated steps from cloud to actionable paragraph.
Residual discipline: investigate, document, report with/without.
Predict in range; say 'associated'; Unit 12 asks when samples speak for the world.

practice.statement: For "daily screen hours vs sleep hours" in teens, which variable goes on x, and what direction do you expect?
practice.solution: Screen hours on x (suspected influencer), sleep on y; expected negative direction.

practice.statement: $\hat{y} = 15 + 2.5x$ predicts quiz score from homework problems done ($x$ range $0$–$20$). Predict for $x = 12$ and interpret the slope.
practice.solution: $\hat{y} = 15 + 30 = 45$. Each extra homework problem predicts $2.5$ more quiz points, on average.

practice.statement: A point has $y = 82$ and $\hat{y} = 74$. Compute the residual and locate the point.
practice.solution: $82 - 74 = +8$: eight above the line — overperformed the trend.

practice.statement: A regression has $r = 0.7$. What fraction of the response's variation does it explain, and where does the rest live?
practice.solution: $r^2 = 0.49$ — about half; the rest is in the residuals (other factors + noise).

practice.statement: The line $\hat{y} = 100 - 4x$ was fit on $x \in [5, 20]$. A colleague requests predictions at $x = 10$ and $x = 50$. Deliver appropriately.
practice.solution: $x = 10$: $\hat{y} = 60$ — legal (in range). $x = 50$: $\hat{y} = -100$ — refuse: far outside the data, and the negative value advertises the nonsense.

practice.statement: Cities' coffee-shop counts correlate with their bookstore counts ($r = 0.85$). Name the most plausible lurking variable.
practice.solution: City SIZE (population): bigger cities have more of everything. Control for population and the correlation should largely dissolve.

practice.statement: A fit's residual plot shows a clear U-shape. What does it mean and what's the fix?
practice.solution: The relationship bends — the line under-predicts at the ends, over-predicts in the middle. Fix: fit a curved model or split the range; the straight line is unfinished.

testYourself.statement: Sketch-describe the expected scatterplot for "car age vs resale price", and predict the sign of $r$.
testYourself.solution: Age on x, price on y: negative direction, roughly linear over normal ages (steepest early), fairly strong — $r$ solidly negative, around $-0.7$ to $-0.9$ in real markets.

testYourself.statement: A line predicting exam scores from sleep hours: $\hat{y} = 40 + 5x$ ($x$ range $4$–$9$, $r = 0.6$). Give: the prediction for $7$ hours, the slope sentence, $r^2$, and the residual for a student who slept $7$ and scored $80$.
testYourself.solution: Prediction: $40 + 35 = 75$. Slope: each extra hour of sleep predicts $5$ more points, on average. $r^2 = 0.36$. Residual: $80 - 75 = +5$ (above the line).

testYourself.statement: Removing one point changes a fit's slope from $2.1$ to $0.4$. What kind of point was it, and what must the report contain?
testYourself.solution: An influential (lever) point — extreme in $x$, seizing the slope. The report shows BOTH fits, the point's investigation (error? different population? real?), and the disposition — never just the prettier slope.

testYourself.statement: Countries' per-capita TV ownership correlates strongly with life expectancy. Does buying TVs extend life? Run the four-suspect lineup.
testYourself.solution: A→B (TVs extend life): implausible mechanism. B→A (long life buys TVs): weak. Lurking C — WEALTH: rich countries afford TVs AND hospitals, sanitation, food security: compelling. Chance: r is stable across years — unlikely. Verdict: wealth-driven association; prescribing televisions treats the correlation, not the cause.

testYourself.statement: Model A predicts delivery times with $r^2 = 0.81$, typical miss $\pm 4$ min, patternless residuals. Model B: $r^2 = 0.88$ but residuals fan wide at long distances. Which ships, and why isn't $r^2$ the whole answer?
testYourself.solution: A ships. B's higher $r^2$ hides heteroscedastic misses — its long-distance predictions (where the business risk lives) are unreliable. $r^2$ is one global number; the residual PLOT shows where a model fails. Fit quality is a portfolio, not a single score.

testYourself.statement: Write the four-sentence 'owner's paragraph' for: plant growth (cm) vs weekly water (L), $\hat{y} = 2 + 3x$, $x \in [1, 6]$, $r = 0.9$, typical miss $\pm 1.5$ cm, residuals healthy, observational data.
testYourself.solution: "Growth tracks watering strongly ($r = 0.9$; the line explains $81\%$ of variation). Each extra weekly liter predicts about $3$ cm more growth, typically within $\pm 1.5$ cm — at $4$ L, expect about $14$ cm. Valid for $1$–$6$ L; beyond that we have no data (and drowning roots won't follow a line). Watering and growth are associated; to prove watering CAUSES growth, randomly assign watering levels next season."
